title: Harris' Conjecture
statement_text: The fractional chromatic number $\chi_f(G)$ can be upper bounded by a function (in fact, even a linear function) of the Hall ratio $\rho(G)$.
context_text: The Hall ratio $\rho(G) = \max_{H \subseteq G} |V(H)|/\alpha(H)$ is a natural lower bound for $\chi_f(G)$. Harris conjectured that the converse holds functionally; this question is also implicit in Johnson Jr [8]. The paper's abstract states it answers this question in the negative.
paper_contribution: Disproves the conjecture by establishing Corollary 3: for every positive integer $c$ there exists a graph with fractional chromatic number at least $c$ and Hall ratio at most $18$.
paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio

title: Blumenthal et al. Question 16
statement_text: Can the fractional chromatic number $\chi_f(G)$ be bounded by a fast-growing function of the Hall ratio $\rho(G)$?
context_text: Blumenthal et al. [2] showed that $\chi_f$ cannot be bounded by any polynomial in $\rho$, but their counterexample graphs still have unbounded Hall ratio, leaving open the possibility of a super-polynomial functional bound. The paper references this as [2, Question 16].
paper_contribution: Resolves the question negatively: Corollary 3 yields graphs with Hall ratio at most $18$ and $\chi_f$ arbitrarily large, so no function of $\rho$ alone can bound $\chi_f$.
notes: Not directly quoted; referenced as [2, Question 16] in the introduction.
paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio

title: Problem 4
statement_text: Determine the smallest function $g : \mathbb{Z}^+ \to \mathbb{R}^+$ such that for every graph $G$, $\chi_f(G) \leq \rho(G)\,g(|V(G)|)$.
context_text: Since $\chi_f(G) = O(\rho(G) \log n)$ follows from a greedy independent-set argument, and the graphs produced by Corollary 3 have doubly exponential size in their fractional chromatic number (giving $g(n) = \Omega(\log\log n)$), the true growth rate of the smallest such $g$ is unknown.
paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio

title: Problem 5
statement_text: Do there exist, for some constant $c > 0$, graphs $G$ of arbitrarily large fractional chromatic number such that $\rho(G) \leq c$ and $\alpha_{\deg_H}(H) \geq |E(H)|/c$ for every $H \subseteq G$?
context_text: To lower-bound $\chi_f$ of the probabilistically constructed graphs in Theorem 1, the authors assign vertex weights proportional to degrees. Problem 5 asks whether such degree-weighted independence certificates can simultaneously be large while the ordinary Hall ratio remains bounded, motivating a search for intermediate weight families $\mathcal{F}$ between $\{0,1\}$-valued weights (Hall ratio) and all non-negative weights (fractional chromatic number).
notes: PDF source — the notation $\alpha_{\deg_H}$ for the degree-weighted maximum independent set weight may be slightly garbled.
paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio

title: Problem 8
statement_text: Is it true that a class $\mathcal{C}$ has bounded expansion if and only if there exists a function $f_6 : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $r \in \mathbb{N}$ and every $G \in \mathcal{C} \mathbin{\triangledown} r$, $\rho(G) \leq f_6(r)$?
context_text: Corollary 7 gives an analogous characterisation of bounded expansion via $\chi_f$ in shallow minors. The forward direction (bounded expansion implies bounded Hall ratio in shallow minors) is clear; the converse argument fails by Theorem 2. Proposition 1 shows a nowhere-dense class with unbounded Hall ratio, but no class with unbounded expansion and bounded Hall ratio in all shallow minors is known.
paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio
